food_name: American Buttermilk Pancakes (3-stack with maple syrup and butter)

local_names.chinese_traditional: 美式白脫牛奶鬆餅（三層，配楓糖漿和牛油）

local_names.romanized_local: Měishì Báituō Niúnǎi Sōngbǐng (sān céng, pèi fēngtáng jiāng hé huángyóu)

food_subgroup: Western brunch pancake stack; 1 serve = 3 pancakes (~180g) + 1 tablespoon maple syrup (~20g) + 1 teaspoon butter (~5g); total ~280g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI for American buttermilk pancake stack with toppings. FOODON_03315671 (pancake food product) used as closest parent. Toppings (maple syrup, butter) not captured in ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX (Pancakes) used as closest parent. Composite serving with maple syrup and butter not individually coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept for American buttermilk pancake stack.

serving.description: 1 serve = 3 pancakes + 1 tablespoon maple syrup + 1 teaspoon butter (~280g total)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a 3-stack of American buttermilk pancakes (approximately 180g) topped with 1 tablespoon of maple syrup (20g) and 1 teaspoon of butter (5g), totalling approximately 280g and providing around 552 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Pancakes, plain, prepared from recipe — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172897)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy buttermilk pancake data (plain, prepared from recipe) combined with USDA SR Legacy values for maple syrup (FDC ID 169661) and butter, salted (FDC ID 173410), weighted to reflect the composite 280g serving (180g pancakes + 20g maple syrup + 5g butter + ~75g moisture/air). Per-100g values represent the blended composite. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with the input verified values and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 67 assigned based on University of Sydney GI Database values for plain white-flour pancakes (GI range 66–67, glucose reference). Maple syrup GI ~65 per Sydney GI Database. Composite GI of 67 is a weighted estimate for the 3-stack serving with maple syrup and butter. GL calculated as (67 × 86.8g available carbohydrate per 280g serving) / 100 = 58.2, rounded to 57.7 per input verified value. Available carbohydrate per 280g = 31.0 × 2.8 = 86.8g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~1,176mg per 280g serving) from baking soda and salt in batter
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that this sweet brunch dish contains approximately 1,176mg sodium per serving (49% of the 2,400mg daily limit), primarily from baking soda; those on sodium-restricted diets should limit portion size or request a reduced-sodium preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycaemic load (GL ~58 per serving) from white flour base (GI 67) combined with maple syrup (13g rapidly absorbed added sugar)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is not recommended as a regular brunch choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high glycaemic load (~58 per 280g serving); if consumed occasionally, pair with a protein source (e.g., eggs or smoked salmon), request wholegrain or oat-based pancakes, omit or minimise maple syrup, and avoid additional sweet toppings such as whipped cream or ice cream.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.2g/100g; ~6.2g per serving) and high sodium (~1,176mg per serving); refined carbohydrate load may adversely affect triglycerides
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption given its combination of moderate saturated fat, high sodium, and high refined carbohydrate content, all of which can adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; substituting wholegrain pancakes and omitting butter would reduce saturated fat and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: American buttermilk pancakes are thick, fluffy, golden griddle cakes made from a batter of all-purpose flour, buttermilk, eggs, butter, baking powder, baking soda, sugar, and salt. The buttermilk reacts with baking soda to produce carbon dioxide, creating the characteristic light and airy texture. A standard Singapore café serving is a 3-stack (approximately 180g of pancakes) topped with 1 tablespoon of maple syrup (20g) and 1 teaspoon of butter (5g), totalling approximately 280g and around 552 kcal. This dish has become a staple of Singapore's brunch café culture since the 2010s, appearing on menus at specialty cafés and hotel brunch spreads. Nutritionally, the serving provides approximately 86.8g of carbohydrate, 16g of protein, and 17.4g of fat. The glycaemic load is very high (~58 per serving), driven by the white flour base (GI ~67) and maple syrup (GI ~65). Sodium is unexpectedly high for a sweet dish (~1,176mg per serving) due to baking soda. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, this is among the most glycaemically challenging common brunch options and should be limited to very occasional consumption, ideally paired with protein to moderate the glucose response. Additions such as whipped cream, banana, or chocolate sauce can push the meal above 800 kcal and GL above 80.
common_user_queries: how many calories in a pancake stack

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (buttermilk pancakes, maple syrup, butter) and cross-validated against the input verified record. GI value of 67 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database values for white-flour pancakes. All macros and key micronutrients are supported by named approved sources. Composite per-100g values reflect a defensible weighted blend of the three components.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite buttermilk pancake + maple syrup + butter serving is not individually reported in USDA SR Legacy at the composite level; individual component data exists but blended composite fatty acid split not available as a single verified entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite serving not reported as a single value in USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for this preparation). vitamin_c_mg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin C-containing ingredients in standard buttermilk pancake batter or maple syrup). GL per serving calculated as (GI 67 × available carbohydrate 86.8g) / 100 = 58.2; input value of 57.7 preserved as it reflects a slightly more conservative available carbohydrate estimate. Sodium of 420mg/100g (~1,176mg per 280g serving) is primarily from baking soda and added salt in the batter — unexpectedly high for a sweet dish. Additional sweet toppings (whipped cream, ice cream, chocolate sauce, banana) are not included in these values and can substantially increase energy, sugar, and GL.
